_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Champaign County, OH. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Champaign County was formed in 1805 from Greene and Franklin counties, with Urbana as county seat. The northernmost and highest-elevation county in the cluster, Champaign County straddles the divide between the Virginia Military District (southern portion) and the Congress Lands survey (northern portion). Records run continuously from formation.

previous_courthouses.location: Urbana, log courthouse on Main Street
Urbana, brick courthouse on Main Street
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1878 building. Records survived transfer.

record_losses.description: Champaign County has experienced no courthouse fire and no significant record loss. The county's elevated position, lack of major river flood risk, and quiet rural character contributed to record preservation. Records run continuously from the 1805 formation. The county straddles two land survey systems — the Virginia Military District in the southern portion and the Congress Lands survey in the northern portion — creating a split in the land record types.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Champaign County's intact records mean that gaps in {ancestor_name}'s paper trail are circumstantial. The dual land survey system means that a deed for land in the southern portion reads differently from a deed in the northern portion — metes and bounds in the VMD, rectangular descriptions in the Congress Lands.
record_losses.source: Middleton, Evan P. History of Champaign County, Ohio. 2 vols. Indianapolis: B. F. Bowen & Co., 1917.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation. Southern townships contain VMD metes-and-bounds land descriptions; northern townships use Congressional rectangular survey.
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Pre-1805 transactions in this territory appear in Greene and Franklin county deed books.
deeds.repository: Champaign County Recorder, Urbana; Ohio History Connection microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com OH land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — probate records from county formation. Estate inventories document the transition from frontier subsistence to commercial agriculture.
wills_estates.gaps: Early loose papers fragile. Bound volumes intact.
wills_estates.repository: Champaign County Probate Court, Urbana; Ohio History Connection

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from county formation
marriages.gaps: Some early marriages may lack licenses. Records are comprehensive after 1830.
marriages.repository: Champaign County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — Court of Common Pleas minutes from formation. Includes early cases involving land disputes between VMD and Congress Lands surveys.
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records incomplete.
court_minutes.repository: Champaign County Clerk of Courts; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Ohio statewide registration began 1908. Rural compliance was slow in Champaign County's farming townships. Some probate court birth/death records from the 1860s-1907.
vital_records.repository: Champaign County Probate Court; Ohio Department of Health; Champaign County Library

substitute_sources.description: Champaign County was the frontier home territory of Simon Kenton, one of the most significant figures in Ohio frontier history. Kenton's personal papers, land transactions, and the early county records document the transition from Native territory to American settlement. Kenton's numerous land speculations and financial difficulties generated extensive court records.
substitute_sources.repository: Ohio History Connection; Champaign County Historical Society; Simon Kenton papers in various repositories
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Kenton was involved in land transactions with many early settlers. Court records of his financial cases name neighbors, creditors, and associates who were among the earliest families in the county. If {ancestor_name} was an early settler, Kenton's business dealings may have touched the family.
substitute_sources.source: Eckert, Allan W. The Frontiersmen: A Narrative. Boston: Little, Brown, 1967.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1810–1940. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) document Champaign County's farming economy in detail.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Agricultural schedules are particularly valuable for Champaign County's homogeneous farming economy.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Champaign County, OH.

substitute_sources.description: Methodist circuit riders served Champaign County from the earliest settlement period. Presbyterian churches served the Virginia-heritage families. Both denominations maintained records that supplement civil documentation.
substitute_sources.repository: West Ohio Conference of the United Methodist Church archives; individual congregations; Ohio History Connection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Circuit rider journals document early settlement communities. Church membership rolls and transfer records track families between congregations.
substitute_sources.source: Sweet, William Warren. Circuit-Rider Days Along the Ohio. New York: Methodist Book Concern, 1923.

narrative_hooks: Champaign County's records run from 1805 without a break — the courthouse on Main Street in Urbana has held the paper trail intact through every year since the county was organized.

The land survey system changes in Champaign County — metes and bounds in the south, rectangular grid in the north — and {ancestor_name}'s deed tells you which system the family's land was in, which in turn reveals whether the land came from a Virginia warrant or a federal land office sale.

Simon Kenton lived and died in Champaign County, and his tangled land dealings touched half the early families — if {ancestor_name} was an early settler, check the court records for Kenton's financial cases, which name neighbors and associates across the county.
Before 1805, Champaign County was part of Greene and Franklin counties — {ancestor_name}'s earliest land records may be filed fifty miles from Urbana, in Xenia or Columbus.
source_references: Middleton, Evan P. History of Champaign County, Ohio. 2 vols. Indianapolis: B. F. Bowen & Co., 1917.
Eckert, Allan W. The Frontiersmen: A Narrative. Boston: Little, Brown, 1967.
Sweet, William Warren. Circuit-Rider Days Along the Ohio. New York: Methodist Book Concern, 1923.
Ohio History Connection. County Records Guide: Champaign County.
